title: Why Spiritual Awakening Often Leads to Changes in Your Relationship with Yourself
meta_description: Discover why spiritual awakening transforms your self relationship. Honest insights on the messy, disorienting process of unfolding into your true self.
og_title: Why Spiritual Awakening Often Leads to Changes in Your Relationship with Yourself
og_description: Discover why spiritual awakening transforms your self relationship. Honest insights on the messy, disorienting process of unfolding into your true self.
body:
You're Not Falling Apart - You're Unfolding

Let me guess what brought you here. You've been feeling off for a while now. Not bad, exactly - just... different. Like the person you used to be doesn't quite fit anymore. Maybe you've lost interest in things that used to light you up. Maybe you've started questioning the life you've built, the relationships you're in, the career that once felt secure. And the loudest voice in your head keeps telling you something's wrong with you. That you're broken. That you're losing it.

Here's what nobody tells you about spiritual awakening: it's not all bliss and light. It's not floating on a cloud of unconditional love while angels sing in the background. It's messy. It's disorienting. And it will absolutely, without question, change your relationship with yourself in ways you never saw coming.

That's what I want to talk about today. Because if you're in the middle of this thing, you need to know: you're not falling apart. You're unfolding. And the discomfort you're feeling? That's the sound of an old version of you dying so a truer one can be born. Right?!

"The self you're losing was never really yours. It was a costume you wore to survive. The self you're becoming has been waiting for you all along."

Why Your Old Self Has to Crumble

Think about who you were five years ago. Ten years ago. The things you believed, the people you tolerated, the stories you told yourself about who you are and what you deserve. Some of that was probably real. But a lot of it? It was armor. It was the version of you that learned to shrink, perform, people-please, or control - whatever kept you safe in a world that didn't always feel safe.

Spiritual awakening doesn't come to make you comfortable. It comes to strip you down. It's like your soul decided you've been wearing a coat that's three sizes too small, and it's time to take it off. But taking it off means feeling the cold air on your skin. It means being raw and exposed for a while.

You might notice yourself pulling away from people who once felt essential. You might lose interest in gossip, drama, or surface-level conversation. You might start feeling physically sick in environments that used to feel normal - crowded spaces, toxic work cultures, even family gatherings. This isn't you becoming antisocial or broken. It's your nervous system recalibrating. It's finally saying, "I can't pretend anymore."

And that's where the shift in your relationship with yourself begins. Because when you stop pretending, you have to face what's underneath. And what's underneath is often a child who learned to be small, a heart that learned to guard itself, a spirit that learned to dim its light just to belong.

The Lonely Middle - Where Most People Get Stuck

There's a phase in spiritual awakening that nobody talks about enough. I call it the lonely middle. It's the space between who you were and who you're becoming, and it feels like standing in a fog with no map. You can't go back - the old version of you feels like a stranger now. But you can't quite see the new version either. You're just... in between.

In this phase, your relationship with yourself gets really complicated. One day you feel expansive and free, like you finally understand something deep and true. The next day you're curled up in bed wondering if you've made it all up, if you're just going through some kind of breakdown, if you'll ever feel normal again.

This is where most people try to jump out of their skin. They want the new self to arrive fully formed, like a butterfly emerging from a cocoon. But here's the thing about cocoons: they're dark. They're tight. You can't see what's happening in there. And the caterpillar doesn't just grow wings - it literally dissolves into goo before it becomes something new.

You're in the goo phase. And the goo phase is where you learn to be with yourself without needing to know who you are yet. That's the real work. Can you sit in the not-knowing? Can you let yourself be undefined? Can you offer yourself the same patience you'd offer a friend who's going through a hard time?

Most of us can't. We rush. We try to fix, label, diagnose, or bypass the discomfort. We reach for spiritual books, courses, and practices that promise to get us to the "enlightened" version faster. But the fastest way through is actually the slowest: staying present with exactly where you are, even when where you are feels like nowhere.

How Your Inner Critic Gets Loud Right Before It Dies

Here's something that catches a lot of people off guard. Right when you start to wake up, your inner critic goes into overdrive. It's like it knows it's about to be fired, so it pulls out all the stops. You'll hear things like:

  • "Who do you think you are?"
  • "You're just making this up for attention."
  • "You're too sensitive. Toughen up."
  • "You're not spiritual enough. You're doing it wrong."
  • "This is just your ego trying to be special."

Sound familiar? Good. That means you're on track. Because that voice? That's not you. That's the internalized voice of every person, system, and experience that taught you to stay small. It's the ghost of your conditioning, and it's thrashing because it's dying.

Your relationship with yourself shifts dramatically when you learn to recognize this voice for what it is: a survival mechanism, not a truth-teller. You stop believing everything it says. You start talking back. You start saying, "I hear you, but I don't agree."

And eventually, you start to notice that underneath the critic's noise, there's a much quieter voice. A voice that doesn't judge. A voice that doesn't need you to be anything other than what you are in this moment. That voice is your intuition. That voice is your true self. And the more you listen to it, the less power the critic has.

One of the most helpful tools I've found for navigating this part of the journey is The Untethered Soul by Michael A. Singer. It's a direct, no-nonsense guide to unhooking from the voice in your head and finding the awareness behind it. If you haven't read it yet, I'd highly recommend it. See on Amazon (paid link)

You Start Treating Your Body Like a Temple - Or a War Zone

One of the most physical ways spiritual awakening changes your relationship with yourself is through your body. You might start noticing things you never paid attention to before. How certain foods make you feel heavy or foggy. How certain people drain your energy. How certain environments make your skin crawl.

This is your sensitivity expanding. And it can feel overwhelming at first. You might go through a phase where you can't tolerate caffeine, alcohol, or processed food. You might start having vivid dreams or feeling energy shifts in your body - tingling, heat, pressure. You might cry for no reason or feel waves of emotion that don't seem to belong to any specific memory.

This is where you have a choice. You can fight it, label it as a problem, and try to numb it. Or you can learn to work with it. You can start treating your body as a messenger instead of an inconvenience.

When I work with people going through this, I always tell them: your body is not betraying you. It's waking up. And waking up is uncomfortable. But the more you resist the discomfort, the longer it lasts. The more you surrender to it, the faster it moves through you.

Start simple. Drink more water. Get outside. Move your body in ways that feel good, not punishing. Rest when you need to. And for heaven's sake, stop judging yourself for being tired. You're literally rebuilding yourself from the inside out. That takes energy.

The Death of the People-Pleaser Self

Here's a hard truth: spiritual awakening will likely kill your people-pleasing tendencies. Not gently, either. It will rip them out by the roots.

You'll start noticing how much of your energy you've given away trying to be liked, approved of, or needed. You'll see the relationships that were built on you showing up as a version of yourself that wasn't real. And you'll feel a deep, almost physical revulsion at the thought of continuing to do that.

This can be terrifying if your sense of self has been built around being "good," "helpful," or "nice." Who are you if you're not the one everyone leans on? Who are you if you stop over-functioning for other people?

The answer is: you get to find out. And that's actually the gift. You get to discover what you want, what you need, what you value - separate from what anyone else expects of you. You get to set boundaries without apologizing. You get to say no without explaining. You get to prioritize your own well-being without guilt.

This is where your relationship with yourself transforms from a transactional one - what can I offer to be loved? - to a sacred one - what do I need to be whole? And that shift changes everything.

Learning to Trust Yourself Again (Or for the First Time)

One of the most painful parts of spiritual awakening is the loss of trust. You trusted the life you built. You trusted the people around you. You trusted your own decisions. And now, all of that feels shaky. You don't know what's real anymore. You don't know who you can count on. You don't even know if you can count on yourself.

This is normal. And it's temporary. But you have to go through the distrust to get to a deeper kind of trust.

The trust you're losing is trust in the false self - the constructed identity that was built on fear, control, and external validation. That self was never trustworthy because it was never real. It was a performance.

The trust you're building now is trust in your own intuition. In your own inner knowing. In the part of you that doesn't need to have all the answers but knows how to take the next right step.

This kind of trust isn't built overnight. It's built in small moments. When you honor a gut feeling even though it doesn't make logical sense. When you say no to something that drains you, even though it disappoints someone. When you choose rest over productivity, even though the world tells you you should be doing more.

Every time you listen to that quiet inner voice and act on it, you're building trust. Every time you keep a promise to yourself, you're building trust. Every time you choose compassion over criticism in your own mind, you're building trust.

And that trust becomes the foundation of everything. It's the ground you stand on when life gets hard. It's the anchor that keeps you from being swept away by other people's opinions or the chaos of the world.

If you're struggling with this part - and most people do - I'd suggest picking up The Power of Now by Eckhart Tolle. It's a classic for a reason. It'll help you understand why your mind keeps pulling you into doubt and how to find the stillness underneath. See on Amazon (paid link)

You Stop Chasing and Start Receiving

Here's a shift that takes most people by surprise. Before awakening, your relationship with yourself was probably driven by lack. You felt incomplete, so you chased things to fill the hole - relationships, achievements, possessions, approval. You were always reaching for something outside yourself to feel whole.

After awakening, that changes. Not because you suddenly have everything you want, but because you start to realize that you already are whole. The hole was never real. It was just the absence of self-awareness.

This doesn't mean you stop wanting things. It means you want them from a different place. You want relationships, but not to complete you - to complement you. You want work, but not to prove your worth - to express your gifts. You want experiences, but not to distract you - to deepen you.

You stop chasing. You start receiving. And receiving requires a different kind of relationship with yourself. It requires openness. It requires worthiness. It requires you to believe that you deserve good things without having to earn them.

This is hard for people who've spent their whole lives earning. But it's also deeply freeing. You get to relax. You get to trust that life is supporting you, even when you can't see the evidence. You get to let go of the exhausting need to control everything.

What's on the Other Side

I want to give you some hope here, because I know this process can feel endless. But there is another side. And on the other side, your relationship with yourself looks completely different.

You don't hate yourself anymore. You don't constantly criticize yourself. You don't abandon yourself to please others. You don't need to be perfect to feel okay.

Instead, you have a quiet, steady sense of your own worth. You know who you are - not as a fixed identity, but as a presence. You trust yourself to handle what comes. You're kind to yourself when you struggle. You celebrate yourself when you grow.

You become your own best friend. Your own safe place. Your own home.

And that changes everything. Because when you have that kind of relationship with yourself, you stop needing other people to fill you up. You stop looking for salvation outside yourself. You stop giving your power away. You become the source of your own love, your own security, your own peace.

Does that mean you don't want connection? No. It means you want connection from a place of fullness, not emptiness. And that kind of connection is infinitely more satisfying.

If you're ready to go deeper into this process, You Are a Badass by Jen Sincero is a great companion. It's funny, direct, and will help you stop taking yourself so seriously while you're doing this deep inner work. See on Amazon (paid link)

A Few Practical Things That Help

Before I wrap this up, I want to give you some practical handles to hold onto. Because I know that all this talk about dissolving and goo and new selves can feel abstract. Here's what helps in the day-to-day:

  • Stop comparing your insides to other people's outsides. Social media will kill your growth. Limit it. Seriously.
  • Journal, but don't force it. Write when you need to. Sometimes just three sentences is enough to untangle a knot.
  • Spend time alone on purpose. Not because you're hiding, but because you're building a relationship with yourself. Go for walks. Sit in silence. Learn to enjoy your own company.
  • Let yourself not know. You don't need to have a five-year plan right now. You don't need to know who you're becoming. Just be in the becoming.
  • Be gentle with your past self. You did the best you could with what you knew. Forgive yourself for the ways you coped. You were surviving.
  • Find your people. Not everyone will understand what you're going through. That's okay. Find the ones who do. They exist.

You're Not Broken - You're Being Remade

I'll leave you with this. Your spiritual awakening is not a sign that something is wrong with you. It's a sign that something is right. Your soul is ready for more. Your heart is ready to expand. Your life is ready to align with something deeper.

The changes in your relationship with yourself are not a problem to fix. They're a process to trust. You're learning to love yourself in a way you never have before. You're learning to see yourself clearly, without the filters of fear and conditioning. You're learning to be at home in your own skin.

And that's not something to rush. It's something to honor.

So take a breath. Put your hand on your heart. And tell yourself the truth: I am not broken. I am being remade. And that is exactly as it should be.

If this article resonated with you and you're ready to go deeper into understanding your awakening process, I've put together a short assessment that can help you see where you are in the journey and what you need most right now. Take the free assessment here.
